topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, acidic knot forming just beneath my ribs. It was a formal notification: my piece, the one I poured months of sleepless nights into, had been published under the name of a complete stranger. I stared at the screen, the pixels blurring into meaningless shapes. A slow, burning flush crept up my neck, making my skin feel tight and hot. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. Every nerve ending felt frayed, buzzing with a sharp, unwelcome energy. I walked to the window, watching the indifferent city lights, feeling a profound sense of being utterly undermined, a raw, bitter resentment pooling in my chest.

[story 2]

When I finally opened the file, the words swam before my eyes, distorted and alien. The familiar cadence of my arguments, my unique perspective—all belonging to someone else. A cold, creeping numbness started in my fingertips and spread inward, leaving me feeling hollowed out. I reread the attribution, the borrowed identity mocking me. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles strain beneath my skin. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small room, my movements jerky and agitated. I wanted to scream, to tear the publication apart, but the sound caught in my throat, replaced by a dry, rasping silence. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating, heavy with unspoken anger.

[story 3]

I read the confirmation twice, my breath catching in shallow gasps. The realization hit me with brutal clarity—my intellectual property, my voice, was being appropriated. A wave of intense frustration washed over me, leaving me trembling slightly. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white, the pressure a desperate attempt to ground myself against the rising tide of corrosive feeling. I started to type furiously, not with clarity, but with a desperate, almost violent energy, trying to find a foothold in the wreckage. My voice, when I finally spoke into the empty room, was low and dangerously steady, laced with a brittle edge that promised retribution.
